We benchmark the proposed system against two datasets: a synthetically generated event-based version of the Sign Language MNIST dataset and a natively recorded ASL-DVS dataset, whilst providing a comprehensive overview of Sign-language recognition and related work. This work yields competitive performance in simulation (92.27%) and comparable performance on neuromorphic hardware deployment (83.1%), while achieving the most energy-efficient architecture (0.565 mW) and low latency (3 ms) across all benchmarked approaches. Despite its compact design, the system demonstrates the suitability of task-dependent visual attention applications for edge deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a low-latency and energy-efficient neuromorphic architecture for ASL fingerspelling recognition leveraging event-based sensing and processing.
AI.motivation: Existing sign-language recognition methods struggle with high latency and power consumption, posing challenges for real-time applications. The paper aims to resolve these issues by exploring neuromorphic sensing and computation.
AI.method: The study develops an end-to-end neuromorphic system with spiking visual attention for region extraction and a compact spiking neural network, implemented on the SpiNNaker platform.
AI.result: The system achieves competitive performance in simulation (92.27%) and hardware deployment (83.1%), with notable energy efficiency (0.565 mW) and low latency (3 ms).
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture is suitable for energy-efficient, real-time sign-language recognition, showing promise for edge computing applications while maintaining performance.

title: SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking
comment: Accepted at IEEE/IFIP WiOpt Workshop, Columbus, OH, USA, June 2026
summary: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SANEmerg, a framework for multi-agent emergent communication in AI-native networks. It improves task accuracy while reducing bandwidth and computational costs.
AI.motivation: Traditional networking paradigms fail to efficiently support large-scale AI-native ecosystems due to their rigid decoupling of communication and computation. This paper aims to develop dynamic, efficient communication protocols for collaborative AI agents.
AI.method: The SANEmerg framework enables emergent communication between agents through an importance-filter for prioritizing key messages and a complexity-regularizer based on the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle.
AI.result: SANEmerg outperforms current solutions, achieving better task accuracy with reduced bandwidth and computational demands, as demonstrated by extensive prototyping and testing.
AI.conclusion: SANEmerg effectively addresses the inefficiencies in agent communication within bandwidth-limited environments, enabling a more seamless realization of semantic-aware AgentNet systems.

title: Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding
comment: 18 pages, 15 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing (TMC) (under review)
summary: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CE-FI, a federated inference framework that allows pretrained models to cooperate while preserving privacy, outperforming solo inference and matching traditional federated methods under certain conditions.
AI.motivation: To enable cooperative inference across heterogeneous and independently deployed machine learning models without sharing sensitive data or assuming common encoders.
AI.method: Developed CE-FI with a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer for aligning heterogeneous representations and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer for prediction. These components are trained using shared unlabeled data.
AI.result: CE-FI outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to federated methods on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, with adaptability across text and time-series tasks.
AI.conclusion: CE-FI facilitates privacy-respecting cooperative inference in distributed settings, though its performance is influenced by representation alignment strategies.

title: Parser agreement and disagreement in L2 Korean UD: Implications for human-in-the-loop annotation
comment: To be published in the 20th Linguistic Annotation Workshop
summary: We propose a simplified human-in-the-loop workflow for second language (L2) Korean morphosyntactic annotation by leveraging agreement between two domain-adapted parsers. We first evaluate whether parser agreement can serve as a proxy for annotation correctness by comparing it with independent human judgments. The results show strong correspondence between parser and human judgments, supporting the feasibility of semi-automatic L2-Korean UD annotation. Further analysis demonstrates that parser disagreements cluster in linguistically predictable domains such as grammatical-relation distinctions and clause-boundary ambiguity. While many disagreement cases are tractable for iterative model refinement, others reflect deeper representational challenges inherent in parsing and tagging L2-Korean corpora.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a semi-automatic method for second language Korean morphosyntactic annotation by leveraging parser agreement, demonstrating its alignment with human judgments.
AI.motivation: To streamline and enhance the accuracy of second language Korean morphosyntactic annotation using a semi-automatic workflow.
AI.method: The approach involves leveraging agreement between two domain-adapted parsers and comparing their annotations against independent human judgments to evaluate correctness.
AI.result: Strong correspondence was observed between parser agreement and human judgments, validating the semi-automatic annotation feasibility. Additionally, disagreement analysis revealed linguistically predictable patterns.
AI.conclusion: Semi-automatic annotation for L2-Korean is viable, though deeper representational challenges in parsing L2-Korean corpora remain to be addressed.

title: Adding Thermal Awareness to Visual Systems in Real-Time via Distilled Diffusion Models
summary: Purely RGB-based vision models often fail to provide reliable cues in challenging scenarios such as nighttime and fog, leading to degraded performance and safety risks. Infrared imaging captures heat-emitting sources and provides critical complementary information, but existing high-fidelity fusion methods suffer from prohibitive latency, rendering them impractical for real-time edge deployment. To address this, we propose FusionProxy, a real-time image fusion module designed as a fully independent, plug-and-play component with diffusion level quality. FusionProxy exploits two complementary statistics of a teacher sample ensemble: per-pixel variance in raw image space, used to weight pixel-level supervision, and per-pixel variance inside frozen foundation backbones, used to route feature-level alignment spatially. Once trained, FusionProxy can be directly integrated into any visual perception system without joint optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on static recognition tasks and significantly enhances robustness in dynamic tasks, including closed-loop autonomous driving. Crucially, FusionProxy achieves real-time inference speeds on diverse platforms, from high-end GPUs to commodity hardware, providing a flexible and generalizable solution for all-day perception.

AI.tldr: FusionProxy is a real-time image fusion module integrating RGB and infrared data for enhanced visual perception, ensuring robust performance and compatibility for dynamic tasks such as autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: RGB-only vision models struggle under challenging visibility conditions like nighttime and fog. Infrared imaging offers complementary information but existing fusion methods have high latency, hindering real-time applications.
AI.method: FusionProxy leverages two key statistics from teacher sample ensembles—per-pixel variance in raw images (for pixel-level supervision) and variance in feature backbones (for spatial alignment). It is a plug-and-play module requiring no joint optimization with host systems.
AI.result: FusionProxy outperforms current methods in static recognition and dynamic scenarios, offering robust real-time performance on hardware ranging from high-end GPUs to commodity devices.
AI.conclusion: FusionProxy provides a practical and generalizable solution for real-time visual perception across diverse environments, bridging RGB and infrared modalities effectively for tasks like autonomous driving.

title: XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction
summary: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

AI.tldr: The paper introduces XDecomposer, a framework for analyzing multiphase powder XRD patterns without requiring prior knowledge or structural templates, achieving high accuracy and adaptability.
AI.motivation: Identifying structures in multiphase powder XRD patterns remains a challenge due to the complexity of real-world mixtures, and existing approaches often fail in multiphase contexts.
AI.method: XDecomposer is a prior-free, set prediction framework that generates phase-resolved components, mixture proportions, and structural representations through a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism.
AI.result: The framework significantly improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification in diverse chemical systems, demonstrating strong generalization to unseen mixtures in both simulated and experimental datasets.
AI.conclusion: XDecomposer offers a practical, data-driven solution for multiphase XRD analysis, eliminating the need for prior-guided, iterative phase matching and opening up possibilities for complex mixture analysis.

title: WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers
summary: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques existing machine learning-based primal warm-start methods for AC Optimal Power Flow, arguing that their reported iteration reductions against IPOPT are misleading due to an inappropriate baseline. It introduces WARP, a model that predicts the full interior-point state, achieving significant iteration reductions.
AI.motivation: Current methods aimed at reducing iterations in solving AC-OPF using primal warm-starts are flawed due to reliance on a suboptimal baseline for comparison. This calls for a more accurate and generalized approach to optimize solver performance.
AI.method: The paper identifies flaws in current evaluation protocols, conducts oracle experiments to explore the impact of predicting full primal-dual-barrier states, and introduces WARP, a network designed to predict these states while handling topology variations.
AI.result: WARP achieves a 76% reduction in IPOPT iterations and provides robust performance even under topology variations without requiring retraining.
AI.conclusion: Primal-only warm-start methods are limited in addressing the needs of AC-OPF tasks. Predicting the full primal-dual-barrier state is key to significant solver performance improvements, and WARP demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of this approach.

title: Cited but Not Verified: Parsing and Evaluating Source Attribution in LLM Deep Research Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) power deep research agents that synthesize information from hundreds of web sources into cited reports, yet these citations cannot be reliably verified. Current approaches either trust models to self-cite accurately, risking bias, or employ retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that does not validate source accessibility, relevance, or factual consistency. We introduce the first source attribution evaluation framework that uses a reproducible AST parser to extract and evaluate inline citations from LLM-generated Markdown reports at scale. Unlike methods that verify claims in isolation, our framework closes the loop by retrieving the actual cited content, enabling human or model evaluators to judge each citation against its source. Citations are evaluated along three dimensions. (1) Link Works verifies URL accessibility, (2) Relevant Content measures topical alignment, and (3) Fact Check validates factual accuracy against source content. We benchmark 14 closed-source and open-source LLMs across three evaluation dimensions using rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge evaluators calibrated through human review. Our results reveal that even the strongest frontier models maintain link validity above 94% and relevance above 80%, yet achieve only 39-77% factual accuracy, while fewer than half of open-source models successfully generate cited reports in a one-shot setting. Ablation studies on research depth show that Fact Check accuracy drops by approximately 42% on average across two frontier models as tool calls scale from 2 to 150, demonstrating that more retrieval does not produce more accurate citations. These findings reveal a critical disconnect between surface-level citation quality and factual reliability, and our framework provides the evaluation infrastructure to assess the disconnect.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new evaluation framework for analyzing source attributions in LLM-generated reports, emphasizing accessibility, relevance, and factual accuracy of citations.
AI.motivation: Citations in LLM-generated content are often unreliable, either trusting biased models or employing retrieval-augmented techniques that fail to validate sources properly.
AI.method: The authors introduce an AST parser-based framework to retrieve and evaluate inline citations on parameters like link accessibility, content relevance, and factual consistency, using both human and model judges.
AI.result: The study benchmarks LLM performance, finding high link validity and relevance but low factual accuracy in citations, revealing issues as retrieval depth increases.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights a gap between surface-level quality and factual reliability of citations in LLMs, offering tools to scrutinize and improve attribution accuracy.

title: T2I-VeRW: Part-level Fine-grained Perception for Text-to-Image Vehicle Retrieval
summary: Vehicle Re-identification (Re-ID) aims to retrieve the most similar image to a given query from images captured by non-overlapping cameras. Extending vehicle Re-ID from image-only queries to text-based queries enables retrieval in real-world scenarios where only a witness description of the target vehicle is available. In this paper, we propose PFCVR, a Part-level Fine-grained Cross-modal Vehicle Retrieval model for text-to-image vehicle re-identification. PFCVR constructs locally paired images and texts at the part level and introduces learnable part-query tokens that aggregate both part-specific and full-sentence context before aligning with visual part features. On top of this explicit local alignment, a bi-directional mask recovery module lets each modality reconstruct its masked content under the guidance of the other, implicitly bridging local correspondences into global feature alignment. Furthermore, we construct a new large-scale dataset called T2I-VeRW, which contains 14,668 images covering 1,796 vehicle identities with fine-grained part-level annotations. Experimental results on the T2I-VeRI dataset show that PFCVR achieves 29.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, improving over the best competing method by +3.7\% percentage points. On the newly proposed T2I-VeRW benchmark, PFCVR achieves 55.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, outperforming a comprehensive set of recent state-of-the-art methods. Source code will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/Neuromorphic_ReID

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PFCVR, a text-to-image vehicle re-identification model, achieving state-of-the-art results across benchmarks with a newly created dataset, T2I-VeRW.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend vehicle re-identification to real-world scenarios by incorporating text-based queries, useful when only witness descriptions are available.
AI.method: The method, PFCVR, enables fine-grained cross-modal retrieval through part-level image-text pairing, learnable part-query tokens for alignment, and a bi-directional mask recovery module to merge local and global alignments. A new dataset, T2I-VeRW, supporting fine-grained annotations, is also introduced.
AI.result: PFCVR achieves 29.2% Rank-1 accuracy on the T2I-VeRI dataset (+3.7% improvement over prior methods) and 55.2% Rank-1 accuracy on the new T2I-VeRW benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PFCVR model demonstrates significant advancements in text-to-image vehicle re-identification, showing enhanced retrieval performance and providing a new dataset for further research.

title: Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value
summary: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a complex risk framework, composed of five engines, tailored to assess and mitigate heterogeneous and composite risks in the Internet of Value (IoV) networks.
AI.motivation: Address the composite risks in IoV networks caused by factors like price, liquidity, sentiment, and policy commitments, rather than risks tied to a single blockchain.
AI.method: Proposes a five-engine risk framework incorporating prediction, verification, sentiment analysis, agentic action constraints, and scenario conversion to forecast and manage risks dynamically.
AI.result: Demonstrated the deployability of the framework through case studies, including a Solana liquidity stress test and prediction-router calibration, with documented outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The risk primitive framework effectively evaluates composite risks in IoV networks, showcasing practicality and falsifiability in real-world experiments.

title: CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CRAFT, a method that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in large language models by learning low-rank interventions instead of updating model weights.
AI.motivation: To address the problem of catastrophic forgetting during the continual adaptation of large language models (LLMs).
AI.method: The proposed CRAFT framework operates in three stages: task routing based on output-divergence, fine-tuning via KL divergence to control forgetting, and merging interventions into shared representations using the same divergence signal.
AI.result: CRAFT demonstrates improved performance and reduced forgetting compared to LoRA-based methods across different benchmarks and model scales, and it shows robustness to task order.
AI.conclusion: By controlling adaptation in the representation space and using output-space divergence as guidance, CRAFT offers a scalable and principled continual learning framework for LLMs.

title: StraTA: Incentivizing Agentic Reinforcement Learning with Strategic Trajectory Abstraction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive agents, but optimizing them for long-horizon decision making remains difficult because current methods are largely purely reactive, which weakens both exploration and credit assignment over extended trajectories. In this work, we present Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (StraTA), a simple framework that introduces an explicit trajectory-level strategy into agentic reinforcement learning (RL). StraTA samples a compact strategy from the initial task state, conditions subsequent actions on that strategy, and trains strategy generation and action execution jointly with a hierarchical GRPO-style rollout design, further enhanced by diverse strategy rollout and critical self-judgment. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld show that StraTA consistently improves both sample efficiency and final performance over strong baselines. StraTA reaches success rates of 93.1% on ALFWorld and 84.2% on WebShop. On SciWorld, StraTA attains a 63.5% overall score, outperforming frontier closed-source models.

AI.tldr: StraTA introduces a novel RL framework for long-horizon decision making in LLMs, improving sample efficiency and performance using trajectory-level strategies.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in optimizing LLMs for long-horizon decision making, such as weak exploration and credit assignment.
AI.method: Developed Strategic Trajectory Abstraction (StraTA), using trajectory-level strategy sampling, hierarchical training, diverse strategy rollout, and critical self-judgment.
AI.result: StraTA achieves high success rates on ALFWorld (93.1%), WebShop (84.2%), and outperforms closed-source models on SciWorld with a 63.5% overall score.
AI.conclusion: StraTA demonstrates improved efficacy in agentic RL, indicating promising advances for interactive LLMs in extended decision-making tasks.

title: PlotPick: AI-powered batch extraction of numerical data from scientific figures
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Software available at https://plotpick.streamlit.app and https://github.com/tommycarstensen/plotpick
summary: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses frequently require numerical data that authors report only as figures, yet manual digitisation is slow and does not scale. We present PlotPick, an open-source tool that uses vision-language models (VLMs) to batch-extract structured tabular data from scientific figures. We evaluate six VLMs from three providers on two established chart-to-table benchmarks (ChartX and PlotQA) and compare against the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot. All six VLMs outperform DePlot on both benchmarks. On ChartX (restricted to bar charts, line charts, box plots, and histograms; n=300), VLMs achieve 88-96% recall versus 71% for DePlot. On PlotQA (n=529), VLMs achieve 86-99% RMSF1 versus 94% for DePlot. The gap is largest on chart types absent from the dedicated models' training data: on box plots, DePlot achieves 24% RMSF1 while VLMs achieve 83-97%. PlotPick is available at https://plotpick.streamlit.app.

AI.tldr: PlotPick, utilizing vision-language models (VLMs), extracts tabular data from scientific figures far more efficiently than manual methods, outperforming the specialized model DePlot on key benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiency of manual data digitization from scientific figures for systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
AI.method: PlotPick leverages six vision-language models (VLMs) to batch-extract structured tabular data from figures and evaluates their performance on two benchmarks against the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot.
AI.result: VLMs outperform DePlot, achieving significantly higher metrics on benchmarks (88-96% recall on ChartX and 86-99% RMSF1 on PlotQA). VLMs particularly excel with chart types like box plots that DePlot struggles with.
AI.conclusion: PlotPick provides an effective, scalable solution for chart data extraction, demonstrating that vision-language models can surpass dedicated models in accuracy and generalization capabilities.

title: Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning
summary: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to achieve machine unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), focusing on forgetting specific visual knowledge while retaining other visual and textual knowledge.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of selectively forgetting target knowledge in MLLMs, while retaining the rest, given the intertwined nature of visual and textual knowledge in these models.
AI.method: The method involves freezing the LLM backbone and fine-tuning the visual module. It introduces (1) Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to separate target and retained knowledge, (2) constraining unlearning within the null space of retained knowledge, and (3) extending the approach to continual unlearning for sequential forgetting requests.
AI.result: Experiments show that the proposed approach effectively balances forgetting target knowledge with retaining non-target and textual knowledge across various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a viable solution for selective and continual unlearning in MLLMs, achieving a trade-off between effective forgetting and robust retention.

title: CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction
summary: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoMemNet, a continual learning framework tailored for traffic prediction in dynamic networks, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing models for spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs struggle to address the dynamic and evolving nature of streaming traffic networks, necessitating a more flexible and efficient approach.
AI.method: CoMemNet employs a dual-branch learning framework: an Online branch for real-time predictions and a momentum-updated Target branch for extracting dynamic contrastive features. It also incorporates a lightweight Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to manage old knowledge without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: CoMemNet delivers superior traffic prediction performance, outperforming existing methods across three large-scale real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively handles dynamic network changes and retains historical knowledge, confirming its significance in traffic prediction tasks; the approach is validated with reproducible results using open-source datasets and code.

title: Beyond Negative Rollouts: Positive-Only Policy Optimization with Implicit Negative Gradients
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), due to the deterministic verification, becomes a dominant paradigm for enhancing the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs). The community witnesses the rapid change from the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), in which GRPO reduces the complicated advantage estimation with simple estimation over grouped positive and negative rollouts. However, we note that negative rollouts may admit no gradation of failure severity, and the combinatorial vastness makes penalizing a few sampled negatives unlikely to cover a meaningful reward signal under sparse binary rewards. In this work, we propose Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a novel RLVR framework in which learning can occur exclusively via online positive rollouts. Specifically, POPO utilizes bounded importance sampling over the positive rollout set. Thus, no disjoint negative rollouts are used for the gradient guidance. We show that implicit negative gradients can emerge naturally through reinforcing the positive probability via rollouts redistribution. Next, POPO stabilizes the policy optimization through two mechanisms. First, it applies a siamese policy network with a momentum-based adaptation law for stabilized policy evolution. Second, we replace the KL-divergence with a bounded similarity penalty term in the siamese representation space. We conduct extensive experiments using publicly available, well-established text-LLM models, e.g., the Qwen family, across all-level mathematical benchmarks. Our experiment demonstrates that POPO achieves performance comparable to, or even superior to GRPO. Notably, we show that POPO can achieve 36.67% in AIME 2025 with Qwen-Math-7B, outperforming GRPO 30.00%. Our ablation and sweep studies further illustrate the necessity and robustness of POPO components.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Positive-Only Policy Optimization (POPO), a framework eliminating the need for negative rollouts in reinforcement learning, achieving superior performance on mathematical benchmarks using LLMs.
AI.motivation: Despite advancements in reinforcement learning approaches like GRPO, current methods struggle with limitations from sparse binary rewards and ineffective penalization of negative rollouts.
AI.method: The POPO framework entirely relies on online positive rollouts, using bounded importance sampling and a siamese policy network with momentum-based adaptation and a bounded similarity penalty in lieu of KL-divergence.
AI.result: Experiments with Qwen-Math-7B model show POPO attaining 36.67% on AIME 2025, outperforming GRPO’s 30.00%, and demonstrating robustness via ablation and sweep studies.
AI.conclusion: POPO effectively stabilizes policy optimization without negative rollouts, providing a robust and more efficient alternative to GRPO in RLVR for LLMs.

title: Domain Generalization through Spatial Relation Induction over Visual Primitives
summary:
Domain generalization requires identifying stable representations that support reliable classification across domains. Most existing methods seek such stability through improving the training process, for example, through model selection strategies, data augmentation, or feature-alignment objectives. Although these strategies can be effective, they leave the representation learning of structural composition implicit, which may limit performance on compositional domain generalization benchmarks.
  In this work, we propose Primitive-Aware Relational Structure for domain gEneralization (PARSE), an image classification framework that factors visual recognition into visual primitives and their relational composition. We represent these compositions using soft binary, ternary, and quaternary predicates over primitive locations, yielding differentiable measures of spatial alignment that can be learned end-to-end. To learn primitives and relational structures jointly, we design an end-to-end architecture with three components: (1) a convolutional neural network (CNN) backbone that extracts general visual features, (2) a concept bottleneck layer that maps these features to primitive heatmaps with differentiable spatial coordinates, and (3) a structural scoring layer that evaluates candidate spatial relations among the detected primitives. We then compute class probability from the joint evidence of its class-specific relational compositions.
  Across CUB-DG and the DomainBed benchmark suite,PARSE improves accuracy by over 4.5 percentage points on CUB-DG and remains competitive with existing DG methods on DomainBed.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PARSE, a framework for domain generalization in image classification, which factors visual recognition into primitives and their spatial relations. It improves results on key benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Identifying stable representations for reliable classification across domains is a challenge in domain generalization, especially for compositional tasks. Existing methods focus on training strategies but overlook explicit structural composition learning.
AI.method: PARSE learns primitives and their spatial relational structures via a CNN backbone, a concept bottleneck layer for primitive heatmaps, and a structural scoring layer. It uses differentiable spatial predicates and evaluates class-specific relational compositions.
AI.result: PARSE achieves over 4.5% accuracy improvement on CUB-DG and remains competitive with top domain generalization methods on DomainBed benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly learning primitive-aware relational structures, PARSE enhances compositional generalization in image classification and improves domain generalization performance.

title: PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning
summary: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an online learning framework to generate personalized summaries of product reviews, addressing user-specific preferences and dynamic changes over time.
AI.motivation: The motivation for this study arises from the challenges consumers face in navigating overwhelming volumes of product reviews, and the limitations of existing summarization systems which fail to capture diverse and evolving user preferences.
AI.method: The proposed method involves using an online learning framework that dynamically learns user preferences through iterative feedback collected directly from the summaries.
AI.result: Using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset in controlled simulations, the study demonstrates that online preference learning enhances alignment with user interests while preserving the quality of summaries.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that dynamic and personalized summaries significantly improve the relevance of product reviews for users, highlighting the effectiveness of integrating user feedback into summary generation.

title: Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Short Communication submitted to Knowledge-Based Systems (Elsevier)
summary: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a behavior-based evaluation framework for agentic stock prediction systems, employing an ensemble of LLM judges for scoring and validation, demonstrating improvements in prediction accuracy and strategy coherence.
AI.motivation: Current stock prediction systems rely on aggregate metrics like MAPE, failing to assess decision-specific qualities, prompting the need for a behavioral evaluation framework to address these gaps.
AI.method: Behavioral traces are logged, segmented into episodes, scored across six dimensions by LLM judges, validated via perturbation experiments, and used in penalty-infused reward fine-tuning cycles.
AI.result: The framework achieves reduced one-day MAPE (11.5% relative reduction), increased directional accuracy (from 71% to 74%), and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement during high-volatility episodes.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral scoring improves stock prediction model performance in offline backtesting, especially in volatile scenarios, though live deployment effects remain unaddressed.

title: EMO: Pretraining Mixture of Experts for Emergent Modularity
summary: Large language models are typically deployed as monolithic systems, requiring the full model even when applications need only a narrow subset of capabilities, e.g., code, math, or domain-specific knowledge. Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) seemingly offer a potential alternative by activating only a subset of experts per input, but in practice, restricting inference to a subset of experts for a given domain leads to severe performance degradation. This limits their practicality in memory-constrained settings, especially as models grow larger and sparser. We introduce EMO, an MoE designed for modularity-the independent use and composition of expert subsets-without requiring human-defined priors. Our key idea is to encourage tokens from similar domains to rely on similar experts. Since tokens within a document often share a domain, EMO restricts them to select experts from a shared pool, while allowing different documents to use different pools. This simple constraint enables coherent expert groupings to emerge during pretraining using document boundaries alone. We pretrain a 1B-active, 14B-total EMO on 1T tokens. As a full model, it matches standard MoE performance. Crucially, it enables selective expert use: retaining only 25% (12.5%) of experts incurs just a 1% (3%) absolute drop, whereas standard MoEs break under the same setting. We further find that expert subsets in EMO specialize at semantic levels (e.g., domains such as math or code), in contrast to the low-level syntactic specialization observed in standard MoEs. Altogether, our results demonstrate a path toward modular, memory-efficient deployment of large, sparse models and open new opportunities for composable architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EMO, a modular Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) approach that enables memory-efficient large language models by grouping experts based on document domains, showing better performance under selective expert usage compared to traditional MoEs.
AI.motivation: Large language models are computationally intensive and often require the entire model even for domain-specific tasks, which is inefficient in memory-constrained scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose EMO, which groups experts based on token similarities within a document, allowing subsets of experts to be activated during inference and minimizing reliance on human-defined priors.
AI.result: EMO matches standard MoE performance in full-model settings while significantly outperforming them under selective expert usage (e.g., retaining only 25% of experts incurs minimal performance drop). EMO experts also specialize in semantic domains like math or code.
AI.conclusion: EMO advances modularity in sparse language models, enabling efficient resource usage and paving the way for composable architectures tailored for specific applications.

title: Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model
summary: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Wisteria, a genomic language model leveraging multi-scale feature learning to analyze DNA sequences.
AI.motivation: There is a need to enhance DNA language models to better capture both local motifs and global dependencies for deciphering regulatory grammar.
AI.method: Wisteria integrates gated dilated convolutions for local motif detection, gated MLPs for global refinement, and introduces Fourier-based attention for frequency domain modeling.
AI.result: Wisteria demonstrates superior performance across diverse benchmarks compared to existing DNA language models.
AI.conclusion: Wisteria successfully unifies modeling of local and global dependencies, advancing DNA sequence analysis significantly.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)
summary: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper explores weak-to-strong generalization, showing it is prevalent in linear logistic regression and doesn't depend on model capacity differences between teacher and student.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the phenomenon of weak-to-strong generalization, particularly in simple settings like linear logistic regression, contrary to prior beliefs about model capacity mismatches.
AI.method: The authors studied standard linear logistic regression under mild data distribution assumptions to investigate weak-to-strong generalization across student-teacher pairs.
AI.result: They found that weak-to-strong generalization occurs for most student-teacher pairs within this simple model setting, challenging prior assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Weak-to-strong generalization is almost inevitable in linear logistic regression, even without model capacity differences, offering insights into its broader applicability.

title: PRAISE: Prefix-Based Rollout Reuse in Agentic Search Training
summary: In agentic search, large language models (LLMs) are trained to perform multi-turn retrieval and reasoning for complex tasks such as multi-hop question answering (QA). However, current search-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods suffer from two core limitations: expensive long-horizon rollouts are under-utilized during training, and supervision is typically available only at the final answer, resulting in severe reward sparsity. We present Prefix-based Rollout reuse for Agentic search with Intermediate Step rEwards (PRAISE), a framework for improving both data efficiency and credit assignment in agentic search training. Given a complete search trajectory, PRAISE extracts prefix states at different search turns, elicits intermediate answers from them, and uses these prefixes both to construct additional training trajectories and to derive step-level rewards from performance differences across prefixes. Our method uses a single shared model for both search policy learning and prefix answer evaluation, enabling joint optimization without extra human annotations or a separate reward model. Experiments on multi-hop QA benchmarks show that PRAISE consistently improves performance over strong baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework called PRAISE to improve multi-turn retrieval and reasoning performance in large language models using prefix-based methods for more efficient and effective training and reward assignment.
AI.motivation: Current search-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods face issues like under-utilization of expensive long-horizon rollouts and reward sparsity due to limited supervision only at the final answer.
AI.method: PRAISE extracts prefix states from complete search trajectories to elicit intermediate answers, construct additional training trajectories, and derive step-level rewards. A shared model is used for both search policy learning and evaluation, avoiding the need for extra annotations or a separate reward model.
AI.result: The proposed PRAISE framework consistently outperforms strong baselines in multi-hop question answering (QA) benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PRAISE addresses challenges in agentic search by enhancing data efficiency and credit assignment, offering a scalable and effective solution for improving complex task performance in large language models.

title: RealCam: Real-Time Novel-View Video Generation with Interactive Camera Control
summary: Camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation enables dynamic viewpoint synthesis from monocular footage, holding immense potential for interactive filmmaking and live broadcasting. However, existing implicit synthesis methods fundamentally rely on non-causal, full-sequence processing and rigid prefix-style temporal concatenation. This architectural paradigm mandates bidirectional attention, resulting in prohibitive computational latency, quadratic complexity scaling, and inherent incompatibility with real-time streaming or variable-length inputs. To overcome these limitations, we introduce \texttt{RealCam}, a novel autoregressive framework for interactive, real-time camera-controlled V2V generation. We first design a high-fidelity teacher model grounded in a \textbf{Cross-frame In-context Learning} paradigm. By interleaving source and target frames into synchronized contextual pairs, our design inherently enables length-agnostic generalization and naturally facilitates causal adaptation, breaking the rigid prefix bottleneck. We then distill this teacher into a few-step causal student via Self-Forcing with Distribution Matching Distillation, enabling efficient, on-the-fly streaming synthesis. Furthermore, to mitigate severe loop inconsistency in closed-loop trajectories, we propose \textbf{Loop-Closed Data Augmentation (LoopAug)}, a novel paradigm that synthesizes globally consistent loop sequences from existing multiview datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \texttt{RealCam} achieves state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency while enabling truly interactive camera control with orders-of-magnitude faster inference than existing paradigms. Our project page is at https://xyc-fly.github.io/RealCam/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RealCam, an autoregressive framework for real-time, camera-controlled video-to-video generation, overcoming limitations of existing methods with a focus on computational efficiency and dynamic adaptability.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing implicit synthesis methods, which rely on non-causal, full-sequence processing with high computational latency and lack of real-time capabilities.
AI.method: RealCam utilizes a cross-frame in-context learning paradigm and distills a high-fidelity teacher model into a causal student via distribution-matching distillation. It also introduces Loop-Closed Data Augmentation for mitigating loop inconsistencies in closed-loop trajectories.
AI.result: The proposed framework offers state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency while enabling orders-of-magnitude faster inference and truly interactive camera control.
AI.conclusion: RealCam achieves significant advancements in visual quality and responsiveness, making a substantial contribution to real-time camera-controlled video-to-video generation.

title: Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery
summary: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses using AI for scientific discovery and introduces the concept of "intentmaking" through interactions with an AI system, AlphaEvolve.
AI.motivation: To explore effective interaction paradigms between scientists and AI systems for advanced scientific problem-solving.
AI.method: Conducting a user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to investigate their workflow and interaction patterns.
AI.result: The study identified a distinct iterative workflow called "intentmaking," which complements sensemaking, highlighting how users refine goals and interpret results through repeated cycles.
AI.conclusion: AI systems should be designed as collaborative tools that foster intentmaking and sensemaking rather than as mere black-box assistants.

title: RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization
summary: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RVPO to address constraint neglect in critic-less RLHF methods by penalizing reward variance for consistent multi-objective performance.
AI.motivation: Critic-less RLHF methods struggle with constraint neglect, where high performance in one objective overshadows failures in others, especially in multi-objective alignment tasks.
AI.method: RVPO employs a risk-sensitive penalty on inter-reward variance using a LogSumExp operator, promoting reward consistency rather than simply maximizing summed rewards.
AI.result: RVPO demonstrated superior performance on HealthBench (0.261 score vs. GDPO's 0.215 at 14B) and stable accuracy on GPQA-Diamond, showcasing improved constraint adherence.
AI.conclusion: Variance regularization can address critical issues in multi-objective RLHF without compromising general performance, making RVPO a promising framework.

title: PersonaGesture: Single-Reference Co-Speech Gesture Personalization for Unseen Speakers
summary: We propose PersonaGesture, a diffusion-based pipeline for single-reference co-speech gesture personalization of unseen speakers. Given target speech and one motion clip from a new speaker, the model must synthesize gestures that follow the new utterance while retaining speaker-specific pose choices, without per-speaker optimization. This setting is useful for avatars and virtual agents, but it is hard because the reference mixes stable speaker habits with utterance-specific trajectories. PersonaGesture consists of two key components, Adaptive Style Infusion (ASI) and Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR), to separate temporal identity evidence from residual statistic correction. A Style Perceiver first encodes the variable-length reference into compact speaker-memory tokens. ASI injects these tokens into denoising through zero-initialized residual cross-attention, enabling style evidence to affect motion formation without replacing the pretrained speech-to-motion prior. Building on this, IDR applies a length-aware diagonal affine map in latent space to correct residual channel-wise moments estimated from the same reference. Across BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS, we evaluate quantitative metrics, reference-identity controls, same-audio diagnostics, qualitative comparisons, and human preference. Experiments show that separating denoising-time speaker memory from conservative post-generation moment correction improves unseen-speaker personalization over collapsed style codes, full-reference attention, and one-clip finetuning. Project: https://xiangyue-zhang.github.io/PersonaGesture.

AI.tldr: PersonaGesture is a new system that personalizes co-speech gestures for unseen speakers using only one reference motion clip without per-speaker optimization.
AI.motivation: The challenge is to synthesize speaker-specific gestures that follow an utterance while preserving their unique style, especially when only a single reference motion clip is given. This is valuable for avatars and virtual agents but is technically difficult.
AI.method: The model incorporates Adaptive Style Infusion (ASI) and Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR). ASI encodes speaker style into memory tokens and uses zero-initialized residual cross-attention for integration. IDR applies a length-aware affine map to fine-tune channel-wise distributions via statistical corrections.
AI.result: The method improves unseen-speaker personalization by separating speaker memory from latent correction. It outperforms other approaches like collapsed style codes and one-clip finetuning across two datasets, BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS.
AI.conclusion: PersonaGesture achieves state-of-the-art unseen speaker gesture personalization without the need for individualized finetuning, enabling significant progress in generating lifelike avatar movements.

title: Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?
comment: ESWC 2026 - 23rd European Semantic Web Conference, May 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

AI.tldr: Digital technologies amplify disparities in Open Access Data across languages; a new categorization of languages in LOD KGs is introduced to support cross-lingual data strategies.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the disparities in Open Access Data availability among languages, particularly focusing on low-resource ones, to avoid excluding communities in the digital transformation.
AI.method: The authors analyzed LOD Knowledge Graphs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) to classify languages into low-, medium-, and high-resource categories using a preliminary multi-level approach.
AI.result: A formal categorization of languages in LOD KGs was proposed, which helps identify and support cross-lingual transfer opportunities.
AI.conclusion: The categorization can be used to mitigate data inequities, fostering inclusion for low-resource languages in the global digital landscape.

title: Full-Spectrum Graph Neural Network: Expressive and Scalable
summary: It is well established that spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) can universally approximate node signals; however, their expressive power remains bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test, which is mirrored in their lack of universality for higher-order signals. To go beyond this bound, we propose the Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN), a second-order generalization of classical spectral GNNs. FSpecGNN advances spectral filtering in two perspectives: (1) it lifts the signal from the node domain to the node-pair domain; and (2) it extends the univariate spectral filter over eigenvalues to a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. We show that classical spectral GNNs arise as a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN, and prove that FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals, the latter being particularly beneficial for heterophilic graph learning. Moreover, FSpecGNN admits scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair-level computations; combined with a low-rank approximation that reduces full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters, it enables learning on large graphs. Empirically, FSpecGNN validates the predicted expressivity and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FSpecGNN, a second-order generalization of spectral graph neural networks that improves expressiveness for higher-order signals by lifting data to the node-pair level and using bivariate spectral filters. It offers scalable implementation and performs well on heterophilic graph tasks.
AI.motivation: Spectral graph neural networks are limited in handling higher-order signal expressivity, bounded by the Weisfeiler-Lehman test. This limitation motivated the authors to extend spectral methods to enhance expressive and scalable capabilities.
AI.method: The authors proposed FSpecGNN to elevate signals to the node-pair domain and apply bivariate spectral filters over eigenvalue pairs for better expressiveness. Scalable implementations and low-rank approximations were introduced to handle large graphs efficiently.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show that FSpecGNN achieves superior expressiveness and strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks while being scalable for large graph computations.
AI.conclusion: FSpecGNN enhances classical spectral GNNs, achieving universal approximation for second-order node-pair signals. The combination of theoretical and practical improvements validates its utility in heterophilic graph learning tasks.

title: Arena as Offline Reward: Efficient Fine-Grained Preference Optimization for Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) effectively promotes preference alignment of text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models. To improve computational efficiency, direct preference optimization (DPO), which avoids explicit reward modeling, has been widely studied. However, its reliance on binary feedback limits it to coarse-grained modeling on chosen-rejected pairs, resulting in suboptimal optimization. In this paper, we propose ArenaPO, which leverages Arena scores as offline rewards to provide refined feedback, thus achieving efficient and fine-grained optimization without a reward model. This enables ArenaPO to benefit from both the rich rewards of traditional RLHF and the efficiency of DPO. Specifically, we first construct a model Arena in which each model's capability is represented as a Gaussian distribution, and infer these capabilities by traversing the annotated pairwise preferences. Each output image is treated as a sample from the corresponding capability distribution. Then, for a image pair, conditioned on the two capability distributions and the observed pairwise preference, the absolute quality gap is estimated using latent-variable inference based on truncated normal distribution, which serves as fine-grained feedback during training. It does not require a reward model and can be computed offline, thus introducing no additional training overhead. We conduct ArenaPO training on Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPD v3 datasets, showing that ArenaPO consistently outperforms existing baselines.

AI.tldr: ArenaPO introduces a novel approach to improving text-to-image diffusion model training by integrating fine-grained offline rewards through Arena scores, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of direct preference optimization (DPO), such as reliance on binary feedback and coarse-grained modeling, and achieve more efficient and fine-grained optimization while avoiding the overhead of explicit reward models.
AI.method: They constructed a model Arena where model capabilities are represented as Gaussian distributions. Using latent-variable inference based on truncated normal distributions, they estimate the absolute quality gap in image pairs. This serves as fine-grained feedback, bypassing the reward model and keeping training computationally efficient.
AI.result: ArenaPO was experimentally validated on Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPD v3 datasets, showing consistent performance improvements over existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: ArenaPO effectively combines the rich, fine-grained feedback of RLHF with the efficiency of DPO. Its ability to use offline rewards without additional training overhead makes it a practical and superior alternative for preference alignment in text-to-image diffusion models.

title: In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the disparities in Open Access Data for under-represented languages and proposes strategies to improve language coverage in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the divide between high- and low-resource languages in Open Access Data, ensuring equitable digital participation.
AI.method: The paper analyzes language distribution in LOD KGs using variables like Wikipedia articles and language-tagged entities, and explores cross-lingual strategies like linguistic proximity and analogical reasoning.
AI.result: Insights into language representation and variables in major multilingual LOD KGs are provided, alongside strategies with potential to improve KG completion and language inclusion.
AI.conclusion: Improving KG completion can enhance linguistic inclusivity in Open Access Data, bridging gaps in digital transformation for under-represented languages.

title: Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning
summary: Differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, which is further amplified by DP noise and degrades both stability and accuracy. Existing remedies apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and all communication rounds (or alternate them on a fixed schedule), ignoring both the structural asymmetry between the two LoRA factors and the round-wise dynamics of training. We propose AS-LoRA, an adaptive framework defined by three axes (i) layer-wise freedom, in which each layer independently selects its active component, (ii) round-wise adaptivity, in which the selection updates over communication rounds, and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases solutions toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost. Across GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions, AS-LoRA improves over the federated LoRA baselines by up to $+7.5$ pp on GLUE and $+12.5$ pp on MNLI-mm for example, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation methods at $33\text{--}180 \times$ lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead. Code for the proposed method is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

AI.tldr: AS-LoRA addresses instability and accuracy issues in differentially private federated fine-tuning with LoRA by introducing adaptive layer-wise and round-wise update mechanisms, achieving significant performance gains.
AI.motivation: Address aggregation errors and performance degradation in differentially private federated fine-tuning with LoRA, particularly under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions.
AI.method: AS-LoRA incorporates adaptive layer-wise selection, round-wise adaptivity across communication rounds, and curvature-aware scoring to improve training dynamics while maintaining differential privacy.
